notes: No compression: largest on-disk DB; bigger SSTs evict cache so reads are slower
attributes: compression=none; base cfg compression=lz4; write_buffer_size=64MB; target_file_size_base=64MB; max_background_jobs=4; block_cache=1GB; threads=16; value_size=1KB

notes: Snappy: ~40% smaller DB than none with fast reads
lz4: smallest DB and fastest reads of the compression group
zstd: heavier decompression CPU with little extra space saving at 1KB values so slowest reads of the group
Smallest memtable: most frequent flushes (highest flush count of the group)
Larger memtable roughly halves flush count vs 64MB
Larger memtable: fewer flushes and less total compaction work
Largest memtable: fewest flushes and least compaction but transient space amplification (DB ~2x the 256MB case)
Fewest compaction threads: LSM stays deeper so slowest reads of the group
Baseline background parallelism
More parallel flush/compaction keeps the LSM shallower so faster reads
Most parallel compaction: fastest reads (~+39% vs bgjobs_2)
Block cache smaller than the ~850MB working set so some reads miss to disk
Block cache ~working-set sized so the whole dataset becomes cacheable
Cache exceeds the working set so extra cache gives no real gain (run-to-run noise)
Cache far above the working set: no further read gain
Buffered IO: reads served warm from the OS page cache
Direct IO bypasses the OS page cache making random reads ~4.8x slower on this storage
Level compaction: fully compacted DB and fastest legitimate reads
Universal compaction: fewer passes but ~2x space amplification and slower reads than level
FIFO artifact: drops ~96% of keys by age (only 37220/1000000 found) so the huge ops/sec reflects a near-empty ~38MB DB and is not comparable
